cross_connections.display_label: Ezra Stiles held his hand at death — received a final squeeze
Eulogized at his 1787 deathbed by Yale's Stiles
cross_connections.notes: President Ezra Stiles (HD0346) attended Whittlesey's 1787 deathbed; per the diary record, Stiles took his hand and asked 'Do you feel now the assurance of hope?' and Chauncey rallied his dying strength to answer 'Yes.' Stiles published a eulogy describing him as 'oracular at college' for breadth of learning. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #88.

narrative_blocks.header: 'No more grace than a chair' — Brainerd's verdict
narrative_blocks.detail: Brainerd later repented of the comment. President Ezra Stiles had a different assessment of Whittlesey: 'In literature he was in his day oracular at college, for he taught with facility and success in every branch of knowledge.' Whittlesey had graduated Yale in 1738, continued as a resident graduate on Bishop Berkeley's foundation, and served as a tutor from 1739 to 1745 — the very years of the Awakening's height. His diary entry from this period reflects the opposite of Brainerd's caricature: 'Having repeatedly commended my case to God by prayer... I am as yet too little acquainted with God, the Scriptures, human nature in general, and my own heart in particular to venture to undertake the great and important work of the ministry.' (Whittlesey Genealogy)
narrative_blocks.body: During the Great Awakening of the 1740s, Yale student David Brainerd declared that Chauncey Whittlesey had 'no more grace than a chair.' Yale expelled Brainerd for it. The Whittlesey genealogy records the remark as evidence of the 'rash, pharisaical, and censorious spirit' of the New Light evangelicals — not of Whittelsey's actual faith.

narrative_blocks.header: Ezra Stiles at Whittlesey's deathbed, July 1787
narrative_blocks.detail: Dr. Dana wrote of Whittlesey's death: 'His old age was amiably splendid as a clear setting sun. His past days looked back upon him with a smile of friendship, and the morning of immortal felicity dawning on his soul gloriously irradiated the valley of death.' Whittlesey had served as minister of First Church New Haven for nearly thirty years (1758–1787), after a decade as a New Haven merchant and colonial assemblyman. The handshake with Stiles — one Yale man to another — was his last act of communication. (Whittlesey Genealogy; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: As Whittlesey lay dying in July 1787, his close friend Ezra Stiles — Yale president and Hooker descendant — came to the room. Taking his hand, Stiles asked: 'Do you feel now the assurance of hope? If you would say yes, and cannot speak, answer me by the pressure of your hand.' Whittlesey rallied his dying strength and pressed back.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached to the wartime Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: On May 14, 1778, with the Revolution undecided, Whittelsey delivered the Election Sermon before Connecticut’s General Assembly on II Samuel 23:3-4 — “the importance of religion in the civil Ruler.” The Assembly ordered it printed and sent Samuel Bishop and Eneas Munson to deliver its formal thanks.

bio.bio_blurb: New Haven pastor in the Awakening’s crossfire

bio.bio_blurb: New Haven pastor in the Awakening’s crossfire

sources.page: Entry 88 (under Chauncey Whittlesey, his first wife)

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve documented children with Chauncey Whittlesey
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Newton Whittlesey and her husband Chauncey Whittlesey had twelve documented children between 1754 and 1773 — a high birth count even for the colonial-era marriages of leading New England families. Of the twelve, five died before reaching adulthood: Elizabeth (born 1758, died 1758), Elizabeth (born 1760, died 1760), John Bryan (born 1761, died 1763), plus Newton (died 1785 at thirty-one) and Susannah (died 1797 at thirty-one).

narrative_blocks.header: Two consecutive daughters named Elizabeth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Martha and Chauncey lost two consecutive daughters both named Elizabeth — one born 1758 died the same year, one born 1760 died the same year. The replacement-naming pattern (same first name given to a later child after an earlier one died young) was characteristic of colonial New England families seeking to preserve a chosen name through high infant mortality. A third Elizabeth, born 1773, finally survived to age ninety-three and never married.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Susannah married Judge Dyer White
narrative_blocks.body: Martha and Chauncey's daughter Susannah Whittlesey (1766-1797) married Judge Dyer White — connecting the Whittlesey line into the Connecticut bench. The Whittlesey family across this generation produced lawyers, judges, military officers, and merchants spreading across New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Milford 1729, lived to age eighty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Newton was born January 27, 1729 in Milford Connecticut, daughter of Colonel Roger Newton, and died October 27, 1812 at age eighty-three. She married Chauncey Whittlesey on August 13, 1753 as his second wife — his first wife Elizabeth Whiting had died October 17, 1751. She survived her husband by many years.

bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut woman who lived to ninety-two

career.notes: Established merchant practice in Middletown Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister and brother both married Van Deursens
narrative_blocks.body: Martha married Capt. William Van Deursen of Middletown in September 1778; her younger brother Samuel married William's sister Sarah Van Deursen at the Reformed Dutch Church in New York City that December. Two Whittlesey siblings married two Van Deursen siblings within three months — a tight kinship knot.

career.notes: Worked the Connecticut River trade out of Middletown.
Practiced at New Haven; appointed Justice of the Peace.
Removed with son Isaac Newton; died there March 7, 1838.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1779; attorney in Connecticut, New Jersey, New York, and Indiana.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Van Deursen sister to brother's wife
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Sarah Van Deursen at the Reformed Dutch Church in New York City on December 10, 1778. Sarah was the sister of Capt. William Van Deursen, the Middletown sea captain who had married Samuel's elder sister Martha two months earlier — a double knot tying Whittleseys to Van Deursens.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Van Deursen family of NYC; sister of Capt. William Van Deursen of Middletown Conn. (I00451). Per Whittlesey Genealogy #154 memoranda.

research_sources.page: Entry 154 (under Samuel Whittlesey, his wife) + #154 memoranda

narrative_blocks.header: Brought elderly father west to Carlisle Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: After his mother Sarah Van Deursen Whittlesey died at Watertown New York in April 1814, Isaac Newton followed the western migration to Carlisle Indiana. His widowed father Samuel joined him there, then later moved with him to Vincennes Indiana where Samuel died in 1838.

career.organization: 3rd company, 1st regiment under Gen. David Wooster (siege of Boston)
career.notes: Per Whittlesey Genealogy #155 + Connecticut Men in the Revolution.
Marched from Boston to New York with Gen. Washington; engaged at White Plains, Trenton, Princeton.

military_service.unit: 3rd company, 1st regiment under Gen. David Wooster (1775); Capt. Hale's company, 19th Continental Regiment (1776)
military_service.notes: Per Whittlesey Genealogy #155 + Connecticut Men in the Revolution. AGE ANOMALY: book records enlistment as sergeant May 15, 1775 — would make Charles age 10 (born Oct 18, 1764). Promotion to 2nd lieutenant March 1776 at age 11 also unusual. Possible book error or conflation with another Charles Whittlesey of similar name.

narrative_blocks.header: Marched with Washington from Boston to Princeton
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Whittlesey Genealogy, Charles served as sergeant under Gen. Wooster May to December 1775 (siege of Boston), then as 2nd lieutenant in Capt. Hale's 19th Continental Regiment from March 1776, marching with Washington to the victories at White Plains, Trenton, and Princeton. Whether the dates are accurate or conflate another Charles, the book records his Continental service explicitly.

education.notes: Licensed to preach upon completion. Degree: Theological course.

career.notes: Licensed to preach in 1825; died March 1826 before settling any pulpit.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale Theological Seminary 1825; licensed to preach but died age 25 before settling a pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: She and Judge White died one year apart
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah married Judge Dyer White on March 18, 1791. He died October 2, 1796; exactly one year later, October 2, 1797, Susannah herself died — leaving the calendar marking their parallel passings.

career.notes: Resided New Haven greater part of life.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1791; later US inspector at New York.

career.organization: New York City and New Orleans Louisiana

career.notes: Tipton is the seat of Cedar County Iowa.

narrative_blocks.header: Iowa county clerk died in 1849 cholera year
narrative_blocks.body: William Kerwood was county clerk of Cedar County Iowa when he died at Tipton on September 15, 1849 — the same year cholera swept the Mississippi frontier. He was thirty-seven, having migrated west from New Haven Connecticut along with several Whittlesey siblings.

notable.notable_blurb: Wallingford pastor, Yale trustee, Old Light leader

narrative_blocks.header: He preached his own ordination sermon
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained colleague pastor at Wallingford on May 17, 1710, Whittelsey preached the ordination sermon himself, from 2 Corinthians iv:7 — and stayed in that one pulpit until his death forty-two years later.

narrative_blocks.header: “One of the greatest men in Connecticut”
narrative_blocks.body: So Rev. Dr. Charles Chauncy of Boston remembered him in 1768, adding that no one he knew could express difficult thoughts with such clearness and ease. Ezra Stiles, later Yale’s president, drew up the printed character of him that ran in the Boston Postboy.

narrative_blocks.header: Fourteen times more in servants than books
narrative_blocks.body: His probate inventory showed an estate of about £22,000 — a scant hundred pounds of it in books, and fourteen times that amount in enslaved and mulatto servants. The leading Old Light voice of Connecticut was also one of its larger slaveholders.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
